utterances: Palestinian Salwa Duiabis leads me up the stairs of what was once a lingerie factory.
It's now a Ramallah office building.
On the third floor, she stops.
We're in the old design room.
Used to have a huge cutting table.
I think it was about 10 meters long.
That was 30 years ago.
So now there's a pingpong table here and a couple other tables and lingerie.
Yes.
Yeah.
Well, I think that if there are young people listening to you and me, Michel, they have to define those boundaries that you alluded to earlier.
They really need to walk where they don't belong.
My life's work has been to go around America with a tape recorder trying to become America word for word.
And I've met a lot of people who I disagree with.
I've met a lot of people who you would think I would disagree with.
But I have to say it's enriched my sense of being an American to reach out to people who are very, very different from me.
It's a lot of fun.
So that's the advice that I would give.
That's Anna Deavere Smith.
Her latest project, ""Notes From The Field,"" is available on HBO now.
She was kind enough to speak to us via Skype.
Anna Deavere Smith, thank you so much for speaking with us.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
From the studios of NPR West, this is DAY TO DAY.
I'm Noah Adams.
And I'm Madeleine Brand.
Coming up: Is the Taliban back in power in Afghanistan?
But first.
(Soundbite of recording)
Unidentified Woman #1: Welcome to 1-800-FED-INFO.
That's the telephone number many US veterans are calling today, and they are understandably anxious.
Their personal information was stolen in an apparent burglary at the home of a Department of Veterans Affairs employee.
Social Security numbers of more than 26 million vets were included in the data.
(Soundbite of recording)
Unidentified Woman #1: We believe this theft was a random act and not a deliberate attempt to steal information about veterans.
The VA is actively investigating, but there is no reason to believe any veteran's information will be misused.
Veterans have been steered to this telephone line and to a help site on the Internet to address any concerns of identity theft.
Gordon Lubold is Pentagon correspondent for Army Times publishing.
Mr. Lubold, what, precisely, could somebody have found out about a given veteran?

Well, I think here they could've found out somebody's name, Social Security Number and date of birth and could use it in any number of ways to start a credit card or to get other kind of information, or just build a new credit score for somebody else.
Could be a full-fledged identity theft just from that data?
Correct.
Part of the problem is that you know you might be, as a potential victim, as a veteran, you might be okay next month.
But the problem is what about the month afterwards, or next - six months or next year.
Tell us about the theft itself.
It was a break in at a house?
What was the information doing at a house?
And is there any indication that somebody was looking for it?

I think the employee was a career data analyst with the Department of Veterans Affairs and took the information home to work on it, and then had the misfortune of having his house - his or her house robbed.
A laptop and external hard drive and some coins were stolen.
There's some indication that there's been a rash of burglaries in the area that kind lends some credibility to the idea that this was a random burglary.
But, of course, now it is on the front page of The Washington Post so somebody who stole it can figure out pretty much that they have it.
It could be worth something.

I think it's clear that it's worth a lot to somebody, whether the person who stole it, who might be just looking for television sets and stereos, has any idea how you possibly sell 26 and a half million names, Social Security Numbers and dates of birth.
I don't know, but certainly its valuable data stats.
We heard the telephone help there and there's an Internet help site.
But one thing you could surely do is to keep checking your report, but that costs you some money if you go beyond a couple of times for that.
Is the Pentagon going to reimburse 26.5 million vets?
The Department of Veterans Affairs, who's really in charge of this whole investigation and everything, has said kind of gently, no they're not going to be, you know, offering free credit reports.
That was their first response yesterday when they first announced this problem.
I think that's something that they're going to have to look at, because I think it's an ongoing issue.
Any veterans groups taking a stand on this being alerting their members and fussing?
Certainly.
You know a number of groups here in Washington have done their own kind of notification efforts of their members.
The VA, by the way, is sending out, I think, upwards of 26.5 million letters and getting the word out to the press and other channels.
The IG, the inspector general of the VA had warned of concerns of this kind of compromise in the past.

The VA has made it clear that the employee was not following procedures, however, you know whatever it was that allowed him to walk out the door with a hard drive full of 26.5 million names is probably going to have to be addressed.
Democrats on the Hill are definitely calling for some heads and want a full investigation and people are very upset.
It's also a bit embarrassing because President Bush had created an identity theft task force, which, as I understand, was meeting for the first time yesterday.
This is, you know, obviously front and center.
Gordon Lubold is Pentagon Correspondent for Army Times.
Thank you, sir.
Thank you.
Republicans are smoothing out the details of their tax overhaul legislation.
They're hoping for final passage this month.
They say the changes will lead to more jobs, more investment.
Most economists, though, are questioning the timing.
They say the U.S.
economy is already at near-full employment, and pushing for faster growth in 2018 could backfire.
NPR's Jim Zarroli reports.

Judy, it sounds, at least on the surface, like it might be a good idea to hold someone to the strictest accountability for a crime like this, but you seem to have some reservations about the death penalty as a punishment.
What would those be?
Well, it seems to me some of these values are screwed.
You know, they're not meeting the needs of the public.
Actually, it's strange.
When we went back and looked at the list, there's kind of a gulf.
There are a whole series of books from the 17th century and earlier, and then there's a whole series of books from the 20th century, and there's actually very little from the 18th and 19th.
The reason for that is when we actually went down and wrestled with the meaning of this word greatest, it really produced two kinds of books.

It produced books that were what you might call argument books, which was what a lot of the 20th century books are: scientists who publish elsewhere but who popularize a particular point of view, or people like Rachel Carson who were not scientists but brought scientific issues into the mainstream.
And then books that were published at a time when publishing a book was - that was science publishing.
You know, there were no journals, this would be the time before professionalism.
Of course, the most - if you're surveying all of time, you know, most of of-all-time happened a long time ago.
It's a long time, yeah.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Give me your five - top five picks in this category.
OK, well, it's a little bit of a cheat because the number one is a tie.
We put Darwin at number one, both ""Voyage of the Beagle"" and ""Origin of Species."" And - I mean ""Origin"" is certainly by far sort of the more significant book in its influence.
Voyage"" is probably the more readable.
I mean it's a travelogue of a very interesting kind.
Number two is Isaac Newton's ""Principia,"" which if you've ever gone through a physics or a history science class, you've certainly heard of it.
Right.
You've probably never read it.
Number three...
You can't pronounce it.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Well, yeah, ""Principia"" is what people call it for short because you don't want to say the whole name.
Right, right.

Number four is Galileo with ""Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems,"" typically just called ""Dialogue."" Number five is Copernicus' book, which is commonly referred to as ""De Rev."" Again, because people don't feel like saying the whole title.
Number six is Aristotle's ""Physics."" Number seven is Vesalius' ""On the Fabric of the Human Body,"" which is probably the world's first picture book, or scientific picture book.
Number eight is Albert Einstein's ""Relativity."" It's a popularization of his theories.
Very readable, too, isn't it?
And surprisingly readable.
Yes.
